title: SpaceX Rocket Engineering Fundamentals
subtitle: How orbital rockets actually work — propellants, engines, reusability, and the SpaceX approach that changed launch economics
description: A practical course on rocket engineering through the SpaceX lens. Covers the basic physics of getting to orbit, why rockets stage, how engines actually work, the propellants and their tradeoffs, Falcon 9 and Heavy reusability, Starship's full-stack reusability bet, and the manufacturing and operational disciplines that took launch cost from $20K/kg to under $1K/kg. Built from public SpaceX materials, NASA technical reports, and standard rocket-engineering texts. Not pro-SpaceX cheerleading; not anti-SpaceX either. Just how it works.
audience: Engineers, founders, and curious learners who want a real mental model of orbital rockets through SpaceX's specific lens. No aerospace background required.
prerequisites: Comfort with basic physics concepts (gravity, force, energy)
No aerospace background required — we'll build the concepts from scratch
outcomes: Explain the basic physics of orbital flight and why rockets stage
Understand the Tsiolkovsky equation and what it forces on rocket design
Map the major rocket engine cycles and tradeoffs
Reason about propellant choices (RP-1 vs. methane vs. hydrogen)
Understand Falcon 9 reusability — how booster return actually works
Track Starship's full-stack reusability and its scaling implications
Apply rocket-engineering first principles to evaluate aerospace claims and ventures

modules.title: How Rockets Actually Work
modules.summary: The physics, math, and design decisions that constrain every orbital rocket — Tsiolkovsky equation, staging, gravity losses, and trajectory.

lessons.title: Orbital flight and delta-v
lessons.objective: Understand what reaching orbit actually requires.

lessons.title: The Tsiolkovsky rocket equation
lessons.objective: See the math that constrains every rocket design.

lessons.title: Why rockets stage — and how
lessons.objective: Map the tradeoffs that force multi-stage design.

lessons.title: Trajectory, gravity losses, and ascent profile
lessons.objective: Track how a rocket actually gets to orbit.

modules.title: Engines and Propellants
modules.summary: Rocket engines turn fuel into thrust. The cycle choice, propellant choice, and combustion design dominate everything else.

lessons.title: Engine basics — chambers, nozzles, and specific impulse
lessons.objective: Understand what a rocket engine actually does.

lessons.title: Engine cycles — gas generator, staged combustion, full-flow
lessons.objective: Map the major cycle families and their tradeoffs.

lessons.title: Propellants — RP-1, methane, hydrogen, and the choice
lessons.objective: Understand why SpaceX uses what it uses.

lessons.title: Merlin and Raptor — SpaceX's engine families
lessons.objective: Walk through the two engines that define modern reusable launch.

modules.title: Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy
modules.summary: The first reusable orbital rocket. How booster return works, what reusability actually saves, and the operational rhythm.

lessons.title: Falcon 9 architecture and mission profile
lessons.objective: Map the rocket end-to-end from launch to payload separation.

lessons.title: Booster return — how landing actually works
lessons.objective: Understand the maneuvers, hardware, and software that make recovery possible.

lessons.title: Reusability economics — what's actually saved
lessons.objective: Track where reusability changes launch cost and where it doesn't.

lessons.title: Falcon Heavy and operational cadence
lessons.objective: See the variant strategy and SpaceX's launch rhythm.

modules.title: Starship — The Full-Stack Reusable
modules.summary: Starship is the next leap: full reusability of both stages, methane-fueled Raptors, stainless steel, and a payload class that changes the economics of space.

lessons.title: Starship architecture and design choices
lessons.objective: Walk through the vehicle and the why behind major decisions.

lessons.title: Full reusability — both stages back
lessons.objective: Understand the booster catch and ship landing.

lessons.title: Stainless steel, raptors, and manufacturing approach
lessons.objective: See the materials and production discipline.

lessons.title: Starship missions — Starlink, lunar, Mars, refueling
lessons.objective: Map what Starship is actually for.

modules.title: Operations, Risks, and the Future
modules.summary: How SpaceX actually runs missions, the risks of the model, and where launch is going.

lessons.title: Launch operations — pads, tanks, range, and crew
lessons.objective: Track how a launch actually happens.

lessons.title: Iterative engineering — testing in the open
lessons.objective: Understand SpaceX's development culture and tradeoffs.

lessons.title: Risks — technical, regulatory, geopolitical
lessons.objective: Map where things could go wrong.

lessons.title: The future of launch — competitors and what's next
lessons.objective: See the landscape SpaceX operates in and where it heads next.
